explain: Meiosis is a two-stage cell division that reduces chromosome number by half and produces haploid gametes.
bullet_points: Includes meiosis I and meiosis II.

explain: A haploid cell has one set of chromosomes.
A diploid cell has two sets of chromosomes, one from each parent.
In meiosis I, homologous chromosomes separate, reducing chromosome number by half.
In meiosis II, sister chromatids separate, producing a total of four haploid cells.
bullet_points: Similar to mitosis in that respect.

explain: Crossing over is the exchange of corresponding DNA segments between homologous chromosomes during prophase I of meiosis.
Independent assortment is the random orientation of homologous chromosome pairs during meiosis I, leading to different combinations in gametes.
A gamete is a haploid sex cell, such as a sperm or egg.
Fertilization is the fusion of two haploid gametes to form a diploid zygote.

text: Why does meiosis increase genetic diversity?
explain: Meiosis increases genetic diversity through crossing over and independent assortment, which produce new combinations of alleles.
bullet_points: Crossing over creates new chromosome combinations.
Independent assortment shuffles maternal and paternal chromosomes.

explain: Homologous chromosomes are a pair of chromosomes, one from each parent, that carry the same genes in the same order but may have different alleles.
bullet_points: One maternal and one paternal chromosome.

explain: Sister chromatids are identical copies of a chromosome that are joined together after DNA replication.
bullet_points: Separate in meiosis II and mitosis.

explain: A tetrad is the structure formed when homologous chromosomes pair up during prophase I of meiosis.
A chiasma is the visible point where crossing over occurs between homologous chromatids.
Recombination is the creation of new allele combinations, often through crossing over during meiosis.
A gene is a segment of DNA that contains information for a functional product, such as a protein or functional RNA.
An allele is an alternative version of a gene.
Genotype is the genetic makeup of an organism, often referring to the alleles it carries for a trait.
Phenotype is the observable traits of an organism resulting from genotype and environment.
bullet_points: Examples include eye color or height.

explain: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype when at least one copy is present.
bullet_points: One copy is enough for expression.
Masks a recessive allele in a heterozygote.
Often written with a capital letter.

explain: A recessive allele is expressed in the phenotype only when two copies are present in a diploid organism.
bullet_points: Requires two copies for expression in diploids.
Hidden by a dominant allele in a heterozygote.
Often written with a lowercase letter.

explain: Homozygous means having two identical alleles for a gene.
bullet_points: Can be homozygous dominant or homozygous recessive.

explain: Heterozygous means having two different alleles for a gene.
Segregation is the separation of two alleles for a gene during gamete formation.
A Punnett square is a diagram used to predict the possible genotypes and phenotypes of offspring from a genetic cross.
A monohybrid cross is a genetic cross that examines the inheritance of one trait.
bullet_points: Often uses one gene with two alleles.
Can be modeled with a Punnett square.

explain: A testcross is a cross between an individual with an unknown genotype and a homozygous recessive individual to determine the unknown genotype.
bullet_points: Crosses with a homozygous recessive individual.

explain: Incomplete dominance is inheritance in which the heterozygote has an intermediate phenotype between the two homozygotes.
bullet_points: Example: red and white flowers producing pink flowers.

explain: Codominance is inheritance in which both alleles are fully expressed in the heterozygote.
Multiple alleles means that more than two allele forms exist for a gene in a population.
bullet_points: More than two possible alleles in the population.
Any one individual still carries at most two alleles in diploids.
ABO blood group is an example.

explain: Polygenic inheritance is inheritance in which multiple genes contribute to a single trait.
bullet_points: Examples include height and skin color.

explain: Epistasis occurs when one gene affects the expression of another gene.
bullet_points: One gene influences another gene’s expression.

explain: Linked inheritance occurs when genes located close together on the same chromosome tend to be inherited together.
bullet_points: Genes are on the same chromosome.

explain: Sex-linked inheritance is inheritance of genes located on sex chromosomes, usually the X chromosome in AP Biology examples.
bullet_points: Gene is on a sex chromosome.
Patterns differ between males and females.

explain: A pedigree is a family diagram used to trace the inheritance of a trait across generations.
bullet_points: Useful for identifying dominant, recessive, or sex-linked traits.

explain: Nondisjunction is the failure of chromosomes or sister chromatids to separate properly during cell division.
bullet_points: Can occur in meiosis or mitosis.
Can lead to abnormal chromosome numbers.

text: How can the environment affect phenotype?
explain: The environment can affect phenotype by influencing how genes are expressed or how traits develop.
bullet_points: Phenotype is not determined by genotype alone.
Examples include nutrition, temperature, and sunlight.

explain: Phenotypic plasticity is the ability of one genotype to produce different phenotypes in different environments.
Norm of reaction is the range of phenotypes that a single genotype can produce under different environmental conditions.

text: Why can identical genotypes produce different phenotypes?
explain: Identical genotypes can produce different phenotypes when they develop in different environments or experience different conditions.
bullet_points: Explains differences among genetically similar individuals.

explain: An environmental factor is any outside condition that can influence an organism, such as temperature, diet, or light.
bullet_points: Examples include light, temperature, and nutrients.
